display_title: 1992-01_02 — BOG-SLOG, SWIM, POT-LUCK at ST EDWARDS
body_html:
Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
January - February, 1992

[Logo: Cascade Orienteering Club Seattle, U.S.A.]

BOG-SLOG, SWIM, POT-LUCK at ST EDWARDS

Sunday, December 30

Meet

The twelfth annual Bog Slog will take place next Sunday, December 29 at St Edwards State Park. The park is about one mile south of Kenmore on Juanita Drive (see inset map). Start times are from 10 am to noon, with the start area next to the pool parking lot. Course designer is Don Atkinson, and he promises a challenging course including some parts of the park, that you haven't seen before. The weather is unpredictable, as those of you who braved the snow last year will remember. This year we're hoping for milder weather. Following the meet there is swimming in the park pool, and a party at Debbie Newell's.

Pool

The pool will is open to the general public from 11:00 to 1:30, ($1.90). Bring your own suit and towell. The pool is open for showers only for $1.25.

Holiday Party

[Handwritten note: Hope to see you there!]
There will be a party following the meet at Debbie Newell's. Debbie's house is close to the park. Just go back to Juanita Drive, and north on Juanita Drive to the stoplight at 155th (Arrowhead School). Take Arrowhead Drive, turn left at the second street (154th), go several blocks to 6216 NE 154th - on your left. There will be the usual bog-slog awards, and a festive party. Everyone is invited. Refreshments will be provided, but non-juniors should bring a pot-luck contribution.

[Map Image: St Edwards Site Map showing Juanita Drive, Lake City Way, and 520]

COMING NEXT:

Seward Park - Saturday January 4

Peter Golde (meet director, course designer, and nice guy) has said that the Seward Park Meet this January 4th is going to be exciting. And that means that the courses aikely to be a bit onthe challenging side. This should be an outstanding local meet featuring four interesting courses. And here's some trivia for your; this year is Cascades 15th birthday with our first map being, you guessed it, Seward Park. So, here's hoping you can join Peter and the rest of us on January 4th for a great time. We'll be starting at the large shelter area at the upper parking lot.

Page 2

Mill Creek Ski-O - January 25/26

Ski and Shoe Weekend
COC will be trying an overnight Ski-O this winter. We will stay overnight at Bruce's cabin at Scenic about 5 miles west of Stevens Pass. There will be a Ski-O Saturday at Mill Creek, about 5 miles east of Stevens Pass, and recreational skiing on Sunday (weather permitting, we will try for the hot springs above Scenic). Come for either Saturday or Sunday or stay overnight. Because of the difficult parking situation at both Mill Creek and Scenic, car-pooling is recommended. If you come to the ski-o on your own, you must have a permit to park at Mill Creek Sno-Park which costs $3/day or $20/season. For those staying at the cabin, we will carpool from Scenic to Mill Creek.

The cabin is about a 1/2 mile ski run from the Stevens Pass Highway. Bring a sleeping bag to stay overnight. We will make a toboggan or ski-mobile run in with packs. Saturday dinner ($2) and Sunday breakfast ($1) provided. Lunch fixings available. Soup and coffee at the cabin.

You can do the course on snowshoes if you like; it doesn't require any training and is fun the first time. If you need to rent cross country skis or show shoes, one good place is any REI store. Their rates are: x-c package, $13 a day; snowshoes, $18 a day. There is a $3 per day discount for REI members. Make your reservation early at the Lynnwood, Crossroads, or Downtown store.

You are welcome to come for any or all of the weekend. However, because of parking and the necessity to plan for food, reservations are a necessity. Please call Bruce at 821-6862. We will be at the cabin come rain or storm, however, the meet will depend upon weather conditions. Check the hotline (783-3866) for latest information.

Election Results

Peter Golde, Mike Schuh, Bruce McAlister, Dave Petty and Debbie Newell were elected as 1992 Board Members at the club election on November 2. At the December Board meeting, Peter Golde was elected President for 1992. A list of officers and coordinators appears on page 8

Cascade 1992 Big Events

As you can see from the schedule on page 1, Cascade Orienteering Club will host a slew of in- town events, including an unprecedented " 1992 big four": Drunken Charlie Lake (including a long course) on May 11 or June 13 (tent.); the USOF Convention July 15/17; a 12 hr. ROGAINE at the White Pass area on August 22nd; and the Washington State Championships at Cle Elem Ridge or Moran State Park on September 19th and 20th.

We hope you mark your calendars now for the big four, as they are sure to be both fun and challenging. The next few Bearing 315 issues will have more specifics for these COC hosted events. (We still need course designers and meet directors for these meets, so if you are interested, please call Denise at 322-7797 or Peter at 883-3732; they'd love to hear from you.)

Newsletter Changes Coming!!

Starting with the next issue, Bearing 315 will be a joint newsletter. The format will be much the same, but there will be separate sections for each club news, and combined results (started this issue) and schedules, and shared editing. All northwest orienteers will receive the newsletter. As always, readers comments and articles are appreciated.

APOC 92

APOC 92 will be held May 2-5 in Japan. Application deadline to avoid late fees is January 31. Harvey Friedman has application form if you need a copy.

Orienteering Association of British Canada 1992 Events

The OABC folks were kind enough to invite us to their planning meeting. Besides having a great lunch and trading "O" stories, we meshed schedules in a helpful and enjoyable fashion. The upshot, coordinating schedules sounds like a great idea for an annual tradition.

OABC will be mailing out their complete schedule to the clubs soon, but they were nice enough to fax us a copy in time for the Bering 315 Jan/Feb issue deadline. Looks like a lot of great orienteering north of the border:

Intermediate Orienteering Class

Mike Schuh will once again offer his Intermediate Orienteering Skills class. Intended for orienteers with at least one year's experience who wish to be more "efficient", the class will meet Sunday evenings for two hours for 4 weeks - January 12 and 19, and February 2 and 9. The class will discuss route choices and tactics, using the regular club events held each weekend. Enrollment will be limited to 10 students. Tuition will be $20 per person, $15 for students, and $35 per couple. For more information or to enroll, please contact Mike at 783-3960.

Page 3

Cascade Chatter

by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 15 December 1991. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Martha Anderson, Steve Avolio, Mark Bingham, Rob Blomquist, Boy Scout Troop 352, Jeff Coker, Larry Farrar, Dyche Kinder, Kai Johansen, Jim McCaa, Debbie Newell, Brad Payne, Bo Powell, Tom Slocum, Doug & Katie Sprugel, Susan St. John, Brent Winant. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

News of note: Following up last edition's Cascade Chatter, Peter Bonek and Claudia Kunzel, both members of the Austrian national team, who are studying at UBC for this year have survived their first term there and stopped the evening of the 16th with Anne and me on there way to see more of America and to ski in Utah. Tracking down the rumor that Rasmus Odum and Charlotte Thrane of the Danish national team would be studying at a school somewhere in Washington state; in fact they are studying at Washington State University over in Pullman. We haven't contacted them yet but did note that Rasmus came in 7th in the Seattle Marathon. Doing even better in the Seattle Marathon was Mark Howlett, who came in 3rd with a time of 2:30. Anne York, who works at the National Marine Mammal Laboratory, presented a very well-received paper at the Biennial Marine Mammal Conference held this year in Chicago. During that same 7-8 December weekend, I also was in Chicago to attend my brother's wedding. We enjoyed ourselves at both. I have another one of Craig Brooks' Rupert Ramblings describing his life and times up in Prince Rupert, British Columbia, Canada. I'll be happy to let anyone who wants to read it. We received a Christmas card from Denmark from Finn Arildsen. He extends best wishes to all who remember him. We received our usual IOF holiday greetings card from Sweden. We learned that Per Bengtsson had another tour group heading for the Veterans World Cup in Tasmania in January and the group would be stopping in California 16-21 December where they would attend a warm-up Class B meet in San Diego. COC Equipment Coordinator and course designer for this year's Bog Slog Don Atkinson will also be going to the VWC. He has an ulterior motive because his brother lives there also. Jessica Rykken is back from working the summer in Yukon Flats Wildlife Refuge in Alaska. We probably won't see her at any meets yet since she broke her leg skiing after only being home a few weeks. Bob and Mary Forbes, part of the course design team for the 1990 PNWOF and World Cup are the proud parents of a new 7 lb, 6 oz son, John, who checked in on November 16. Since I've already mentioned 3 of the 4 members of the PNWOF -WC design team (Anne, Bob, Mary and Fred), we have a report about the final member Fred Veler. Fred has married Martha Swift and moved to Seattle. COC founder Len Englund has finished with his chemotherapy and is feeling much better now. We should soon see him at meets again. NEOC (New England Orienteering Club) members Mike Fritz and Vivian Benning and John Rogers and Beth Elliot, as well as Bay Area Orienteering Club members Steve McConnell and (soon) Marci Jaffe.

Remember, the bulk of club memberships are due to expire New Years Eve. If you are in this category, be sure to write your renewal check pronto (or turn it in at the Bog Slog). My memory is not so good anymore, so if anyone has news of interest, write me a note, so I can include it in Cascade Chatter.

Reminiscences of an Experimental College Instructor

by Mike Schuh

The Fall quarter class Experimental College class marked my tenth anniversary of teaching orienteering. Through the years I have learned a lot about sharing my enthusiasm for the sport and have met many wonderful people. You probably know some of them: Barbara Pedersen (1981), Roger Sharp (1983), Kenneth Wiljanen (1983), Ken Knapp (1983), Paul Bullard (1984), Eileen Breseman (1984), Francis Smith (1984), Doug and Katie Sprugel (1985), Steve Layman (1986), Rick Donaldson (1988), Debbie Armstrong (1988), Thurston Miller (1988), and David Soper and Denise Klein (1989). (Perhaps I should start a "Mike Schuh Alumni Association". Then I could get paid an outrageous fee to speak at their annual dinner...) Several of these - Barbara, Roger, Eileen, Francis, Doug, Katie, David, and Denise - have held positions in COC's leadership. Thurston was instrumental in forming the Las Vegas, Nevada, O' club.

The diversity of students taking my class has been amazing - rich and poor, high school dropouts and PhD's, young and old, families, jocks and the not-so-athletic. One of the more heart-warming stories involves a student who told me he really wanted to take the class but couldn't pay (he'd just been laid off). I assured him that he could attend and to pay me whenever he got the chance. The class came and went and I soon forgot about him and the fact that he never paid. Then one day, while walking in Ballard, I'm stopped by this stranger who identifies himself as this student and pays me for the class he'd taken a couple of years earlier. Restores one's faith in humanity... At the other end of the economic scale, my students have included members of the Nordstrom family and an Olympic Gold medal winner. Two Japanese exchange students took the class in the Spring of 1989; in the Fall 1991 class, an equestrian with experience in mounted orienteering was a student. It's been interesting.

My original class outline was derived from an outline published circa 1980 by the US Orienteering Federation's Education Committee. It called for 16 one hour classes, which I combined into 8 two hour classes, with one class per week. In the Spring of 1985 I rewrote the outline, dropping several topics, and shortening the class to just four weeks. Besides an immediate quadrupling of class enrollment, this also lessened instructor burnout and made the whole endeavor a lot more fun.

Initially I taught the class in just the Fall but starting in 1984 have offered it, with a couple of exceptions, in both the Spring and Fall (Summer is too busy to try to teach it - and the forest too thick - while Winter is taken up with the school league). Beginning with the Spring 1989 class, I offered two sections, one in the morning from 10:00 am until noon (as I had done from the beginning), the other from 12:30 pm until 2:30 pm. This has resulted in another 50 percent increase in students. Over the decade, I have had a grand total of 300 students and have given 85 days of instruction.

With the exception of one class taught at Camp Long, all of the classes have been held at Discovery Park. This is a nearly ideal setting for such a class: we can use the visitors center conference room to discuss various techniques and concepts, and then just walk outside and try them. At first I used a simple black and white map of a corner of the park - it was what I could afford. Then in 1982 I produced a color map, with a marked improvement in students' comprehension and interest. This has lead me to advocate color for all orienteering maps, even itty-bitty ones that might otherwise be ignored.

I hope to continue teaching this class for many years. It has been a richly rewarding experience, and I thank all of those students who have made it worthwhile and fun.

Page 4

MEET RESULTS

Western States Championships

Sunol State Park, California
November 23/24
by Harvey Friedman

Leaving Sea-Tac on a rainy 45 degree morning and arriving in San Jose a couple of hours later to 65 degree sunshine was a great start for an O' weekend. Anne York, Knut Olson, and I, Harvey Friedman arose in the wee hours of morning to catch our 7:45 AM flight. We had no problems, picked up our rental car and a standard rental car company map, and navigated ourselves right to Sunol Regional Park and the registration table. We arrived just before 1100 so had enough time to change and copy our control descriptions to our punch cards before trekking for about 50 minutes the 2 kilometers, 700 meters climb to the start. We knew that several club members would be going down Friday evening, but were rather surprised when scanning Saturday's results to see that 18 from the state of Washington plus Alex Kerr from Vancouver attended. Total registration was only about 120 (not counting week-end walk-ons) so we were quite wellrepresented. In fact, of the 18, 6 earned ribbons. Vampire Night O

Hamlin Park, November 2
Cascade Orienteering Club

Meet Director/Timing: Don Atkinson
Course: Debbie Newell
Escort to Ms. Newell and Director of Vampire Operations: Rick Hood
Registration: Mike Schuh, Robert Miller
Control Pickup: Sandy Kish Miller, Rick Hood
Map Update: Bob Williamson

It was a scream; it was truly FunO. With 3 vampires and 20-some orienteers, almost everyone got bitten at least once, with many (including our top finishers) getting bitten twice. The top finishers guessed they punched some 25 to 30 times and the Champ had just gotten a card with 15 minutes to go and only 5 controls; he hustled and stayed off the trails. Kudos to Debbie for course design, as every control was visited almost equally (all controls were punched 7-10 times save for control #14, which was punched only 3 times.) Special thanks to Bob Williamson for updating the map just prior to the meet.

In sum, perfect weather on the course and good pizza and vampire stories afterwards made for a great evening. (Can a summer day squirt-gun vampire O or a Klingon vs. the Federation night O be far off? Let's hope so, but look for the newly established Halloween Vampire O next year.) Oh, next year when a vampire bites someone, it takes-no looking at their control card, seeing too few punches, and then saying, "sorry, I wasn't thirsty."
Rick Hood

Course: 20 controls worth 1 point apiece, mass start, 45 minute time limit, 2 points per minute overtime penalty.

Forest Park - November 17

Sammamish Orienteering Club

Meet Director: Mac MacDonald
Courses: Ben Hall
Registration: Kathy Trost
Start: Ben Hall
Finish: Ben Hall, Mac MacDonald

Sixteen lucky advanced-course orienteers were challenged to participate in a motala at Forest Park in Everett. A motala is an orienteering race in which all participants start at the same time, and each participant runs three to four short courses one after another -- but not necessarily in the same sequence as the other competitors. Each runner begins with one map, then trades it in for another when he/she completes the first course... and so on, until all the courses are completed.

At Forest Park there were four courses -- A, B, C and D -- and so while runner #1 might run the race in sequence ABCD, another might run DCBA, or BACD, etc. Ideally, the course sequences diverge as quickly as possible, so that runners who start the race on the same leg -- say, leg A -- will run a different 2nd leg. In practice, a four-course motala has only 24 discrete course combinations (4 factorial), and half of those have the same 1st/2nd leg combination as the other half... so if there are more than 12 participants, some people will be running the same 2 first legs as some others.

Rather than lose sleep over the combinatorics of motala sequences, I wrote a program to run on a laptop that would, among other things, automate the process of assigning course sequences to runners as they registered, insuring the maximum randomness for a given number of entrants. How well did it work? It doled out sequences CDAB and CDBA to John Rogers and Dave Enger, two people with exceptional and comparable orienteering skills; I couldn't have chosen a worse pair of sequences for these two. This explains why Microsoft returned my resume.

However, the program was useful in other areas -- most notably, in that I could easily take split times for the runners as they entered the map-exchange area in between legs. Motala splits give an interesting view into the mid-race dynamics by providing four mini-race results (see results). Here are some of my observations on that data:

All four legs were roughly 1.4k, for a total distance of just under 6k. John Rogers (a recent transplant from New England and 1990 U.S. Champion in M35), won the race in 50:48, by a scant 26 seconds over Dave Enger. Although John never posted the fastest time for any one leg, he was consistently 2nd or 3rd. Enger lost the race on leg B alone; his ACD time was better than John's. This is

FIRST CLASS POSTAGE

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club January - February, 1992
BOG-SLOG, SWIM, POT-LUCK at ST EDWARDS

The twelfth annual Bog Slog will take place next Sunday, December 29 at St Edwards State Park. The park is about one mile south of Kenmore on Juanita Drive (see inset map). Start times are from 10 am to noon, with the start area next to the pool parking lot. Course designer is Don Atkinson, and he promises a challenging course including some parts of the park, that you haven't seen before. The weather is unpredictable, as those of you who braved the snow last year will remember. This year we're hoping for milder weather. Following the meet there is swimming in the park pool, and a party at Debbie Newell's.
The pool will is open to the general public from 11:00 to 1:30, ($1.90). Bring your own suit and towell. The pool is open for showers only for $1.25.

[Handwritten note: Hope to see you there!] There will be a party following the meet at Debbie Newell's. Debbie's house is close to the park. Just go back to Juanita Drive, and north on Juanita Drive to the stoplight at 155th (Arrowhead School). Take Arrowhead Drive, turn left at the second street (154th), go several blocks to 6216 NE 154th - on your left. There will be the usual bog-slog awards, and a festive party. Everyone is invited. Refreshments will be provided, but non-juniors should bring a pot-luck contribution.
[Map Image: St Edwards Site Map showing Juanita Drive, Lake City Way, and 520]

Peter Golde (meet director, course designer, and nice guy) has said that the Seward Park Meet this January 4th is going to be exciting. And that means that the courses aikely to be a bit onthe challenging side. This should be an outstanding local meet featuring four interesting courses. And here's some trivia for your; this year is Cascades 15th birthday with our first map being, you guessed it, Seward Park. So, here's hoping you can join Peter and the rest of us on January 4th for a great time. We'll be starting at the large shelter area at the upper parking lot.

Ski and Shoe Weekend COC will be trying an overnight Ski-O this winter. We will stay overnight at Bruce's cabin at Scenic about 5 miles west of Stevens Pass. There will be a Ski-O Saturday at Mill Creek, about 5 miles east of Stevens Pass, and recreational skiing on Sunday (weather permitting, we will try for the hot springs above Scenic). Come for either Saturday or Sunday or stay overnight. Because of the difficult parking situation at both Mill Creek and Scenic, car-pooling is recommended. If you come to the ski-o on your own, you must have a permit to park at Mill Creek Sno-Park which costs $3/day or $20/season. For those staying at the cabin, we will carpool from Scenic to Mill Creek.

The cabin is about a 1/2 mile ski run from the Stevens Pass Highway. Bring a sleeping bag to stay overnight. We will make a toboggan or ski-mobile run in with packs. Saturday dinner ($2) and Sunday breakfast ($1) provided. Lunch fixings available. Soup and coffee at the cabin.

You can do the course on snowshoes if you like; it doesn't require any training and is fun the first time. If you need to rent cross country skis or show shoes, one good place is any REI store. Their rates are: x-c package, $13 a day; snowshoes, $18 a day. There is a $3 per day discount for REI members. Make your reservation early at the Lynnwood, Crossroads, or Downtown store.

You are welcome to come for any or all of the weekend. However, because of parking and the necessity to plan for food, reservations are a necessity. Please call Bruce at 821-6862. We will be at the cabin come rain or storm, however, the meet will depend upon weather conditions. Check the hotline (783-3866) for latest information.

Peter Golde, Mike Schuh, Bruce McAlister, Dave Petty and Debbie Newell were elected as 1992 Board Members at the club election on November 2. At the December Board meeting, Peter Golde was elected President for 1992. A list of officers and coordinators appears on page 8

As you can see from the schedule on page 1, Cascade Orienteering Club will host a slew of in- town events, including an unprecedented " 1992 big four": Drunken Charlie Lake (including a long course) on May 11 or June 13 (tent.); the USOF Convention July 15/17; a 12 hr. ROGAINE at the White Pass area on August 22nd; and the Washington State Championships at Cle Elem Ridge or Moran State Park on September 19th and 20th.

We hope you mark your calendars now for the big four, as they are sure to be both fun and challenging. The next few Bearing 315 issues will have more specifics for these COC hosted events. (We still need course designers and meet directors for these meets, so if you are interested, please call Denise at 322-7797 or Peter at 883-3732; they'd love to hear from you.)

Starting with the next issue, Bearing 315 will be a joint newsletter. The format will be much the same, but there will be separate sections for each club news, and combined results (started this issue) and schedules, and shared editing. All northwest orienteers will receive the newsletter. As always, readers comments and articles are appreciated.
APOC 92 will be held May 2-5 in Japan. Application deadline to avoid late fees is January 31. Harvey Friedman has application form if you need a copy.
Orienteering Association of British Canada 1992 Events

The OABC folks were kind enough to invite us to their planning meeting. Besides having a great lunch and trading "O" stories, we meshed schedules in a helpful and enjoyable fashion. The upshot, coordinating schedules sounds like a great idea for an annual tradition.

OABC will be mailing out their complete schedule to the clubs soon, but they were nice enough to fax us a copy in time for the Bering 315 Jan/Feb issue deadline. Looks like a lot of great orienteering north of the border: (all area codes are 604)

Mike Schuh will once again offer his Intermediate Orienteering Skills class. Intended for orienteers with at least one year's experience who wish to be more "efficient", the class will meet Sunday evenings for two hours for 4 weeks - January 12 and 19, and February 2 and 9. The class will discuss route choices and tactics, using the regular club events held each weekend. Enrollment will be limited to 10 students. Tuition will be $20 per person, $15 for students, and $35 per couple. For more information or to enroll, please contact Mike at 783-3960.

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 15 December 1991. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Martha Anderson, Steve Avolio, Mark Bingham, Rob Blomquist, Boy Scout Troop 352, Jeff Coker, Larry Farrar, Dyche Kinder, Kai Johansen, Jim McCaa, Debbie Newell, Brad Payne, Bo Powell, Tom Slocum, Doug & Katie Sprugel, Susan St. John, Brent Winant. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

News of note: Following up last edition's Cascade Chatter, Peter Bonek and Claudia Kunzel, both members of the Austrian national team, who are studying at UBC for this year have survived their first term there and stopped the evening of the 16th with Anne and me on there way to see more of America and to ski in Utah. Tracking down the rumor that Rasmus Odum and Charlotte Thrane of the Danish national team would be studying at a school somewhere in Washington state; in fact they are studying at Washington State University over in Pullman. We haven't contacted them yet but did note that Rasmus came in 7th in the Seattle Marathon. Doing even better in the Seattle Marathon was Mark Howlett, who came in 3rd with a time of 2:30. Anne York, who works at the National Marine Mammal Laboratory, presented a very well-received paper at the Biennial Marine Mammal Conference held this year in Chicago. During that same 7-8 December weekend, I also was in Chicago to attend my brother's wedding. We enjoyed ourselves at both. I have another one of Craig Brooks' Rupert Ramblings describing his life and times up in Prince Rupert, British Columbia, Canada. I'll be happy to let anyone who wants to read it. We received a Christmas card from Denmark from Finn Arildsen. He extends best wishes to all who remember him. We received our usual IOF holiday greetings card from Sweden. We learned that Per Bengtsson had another tour group heading for the Veterans World Cup in Tasmania in January and the group would be stopping in California 16-21 December where they would attend a warm-up Class B meet in San Diego. COC Equipment Coordinator and course designer for this year's Bog Slog Don Atkinson will also be going to the VWC. He has an ulterior motive because his brother lives there also. Jessica Rykken is back from working the summer in Yukon Flats Wildlife Refuge in Alaska. We probably won't see her at any meets yet since she broke her leg skiing after only being home a few weeks. Bob and Mary Forbes, part of the course design team for the 1990 PNWOF and World Cup are the proud parents of a new 7 lb, 6 oz son, John, who checked in on November 16. Since I've already mentioned 3 of the 4 members of the PNWOF -WC design team (Anne, Bob, Mary and Fred), we have a report about the final member Fred Veler. Fred has married Martha Swift and moved to Seattle. COC founder Len Englund has finished with his chemotherapy and is feeling much better now. We should soon see him at meets again. NEOC (New England Orienteering Club) members Mike Fritz and Vivian Benning and John Rogers and Beth Elliot, as well as Bay Area Orienteering Club members Steve McConnell and (soon) Marci Jaffe.

Remember, the bulk of club memberships are due to expire New Years Eve. If you are in this category, be sure to write your renewal check pronto (or turn it in at the Bog Slog). My memory is not so good anymore, so if anyone has news of interest, write me a note, so I can include it in Cascade Chatter.
Reminiscences of an Experimental College Instructor

The Fall quarter class Experimental College class marked my tenth anniversary of teaching orienteering. Through the years I have learned a lot about sharing my enthusiasm for the sport and have met many wonderful people. You probably know some of them: Barbara Pedersen (1981), Roger Sharp (1983), Kenneth Wiljanen (1983), Ken Knapp (1983), Paul Bullard (1984), Eileen Breseman (1984), Francis Smith (1984), Doug and Katie Sprugel (1985), Steve Layman (1986), Rick Donaldson (1988), Debbie Armstrong (1988), Thurston Miller (1988), and David Soper and Denise Klein (1989). (Perhaps I should start a "Mike Schuh Alumni Association". Then I could get paid an outrageous fee to speak at their annual dinner...) Several of these - Barbara, Roger, Eileen, Francis, Doug, Katie, David, and Denise - have held positions in COC's leadership. Thurston was instrumental in forming the Las Vegas, Nevada, O' club.

The diversity of students taking my class has been amazing - rich and poor, high school dropouts and PhD's, young and old, families, jocks and the not-so-athletic. One of the more heart-warming stories involves a student who told me he really wanted to take the class but couldn't pay (he'd just been laid off). I assured him that he could attend and to pay me whenever he got the chance. The class came and went and I soon forgot about him and the fact that he never paid. Then one day, while walking in Ballard, I'm stopped by this stranger who identifies himself as this student and pays me for the class he'd taken a couple of years earlier. Restores one's faith in humanity... At the other end of the economic scale, my students have included members of the Nordstrom family and an Olympic Gold medal winner. Two Japanese exchange students took the class in the Spring of 1989; in the Fall 1991 class, an equestrian with experience in mounted orienteering was a student. It's been interesting.

My original class outline was derived from an outline published circa 1980 by the US Orienteering Federation's Education Committee. It called for 16 one hour classes, which I combined into 8 two hour classes, with one class per week. In the Spring of 1985 I rewrote the outline, dropping several topics, and shortening the class to just four weeks. Besides an immediate quadrupling of class enrollment, this also lessened instructor burnout and made the whole endeavor a lot more fun.

Initially I taught the class in just the Fall but starting in 1984 have offered it, with a couple of exceptions, in both the Spring and Fall (Summer is too busy to try to teach it - and the forest too thick - while Winter is taken up with the school league). Beginning with the Spring 1989 class, I offered two sections, one in the morning from 10:00 am until noon (as I had done from the beginning), the other from 12:30 pm until 2:30 pm. This has resulted in another 50 percent increase in students. Over the decade, I have had a grand total of 300 students and have given 85 days of instruction.

With the exception of one class taught at Camp Long, all of the classes have been held at Discovery Park. This is a nearly ideal setting for such a class: we can use the visitors center conference room to discuss various techniques and concepts, and then just walk outside and try them. At first I used a simple black and white map of a corner of the park - it was what I could afford. Then in 1982 I produced a color map, with a marked improvement in students' comprehension and interest. This has lead me to advocate color for all orienteering maps, even itty-bitty ones that might otherwise be ignored.
I hope to continue teaching this class for many years. It has been a richly rewarding experience, and I thank all of those students who have made it worthwhile and fun.
Sunol State Park, California November 23/24 by Harvey Friedman

Leaving Sea-Tac on a rainy 45 degree morning and arriving in San Jose a couple of hours later to 65 degree sunshine was a great start for an O' weekend. Anne York, Knut Olson, and I, Harvey Friedman arose in the wee hours of morning to catch our 7:45 AM flight. We had no problems, picked up our rental car and a standard rental car company map, and navigated ourselves right to Sunol Regional Park and the registration table. We arrived just before 1100 so had enough time to change and copy our control descriptions to our punch cards before trekking for about 50 minutes the 2 kilometers, 700 meters climb to the start. We knew that several club members would be going down Friday evening, but were rather surprised when scanning Saturday's results to see that 18 from the state of Washington plus Alex Kerr from Vancouver attended. Total registration was only about 120 (not counting week-end walk-ons) so we were quite wellrepresented. In fact, of the 18, 6 earned ribbons. Course, class, name, place, and day 2 time and total time are provided below.
Hamlin Park, November 2 Cascade Orienteering Club

Meet Director/Timing: Don Atkinson Course: Debbie Newell Escort to Ms. Newell and Director of Vampire Operations: Rick Hood Registration: Mike Schuh, Robert Miller Control Pickup: Sandy Kish Miller, Rick Hood Map Update: Bob Williamson

It was a scream; it was truly FunO. With 3 vampires and 20-some orienteers, almost everyone got bitten at least once, with many (including our top finishers) getting bitten twice. The top finishers guessed they punched some 25 to 30 times and the Champ had just gotten a card with 15 minutes to go and only 5 controls; he hustled and stayed off the trails. Kudos to Debbie for course design, as every control was visited almost equally (all controls were punched 7-10 times save for control #14, which was punched only 3 times.) Special thanks to Bob Williamson for updating the map just prior to the meet.

In sum, perfect weather on the course and good pizza and vampire stories afterwards made for a great evening. (Can a summer day squirt-gun vampire O or a Klingon vs. the Federation night O be far off? Let's hope so, but look for the newly established Halloween Vampire O next year.) Oh, next year when a vampire bites someone, it takes-no looking at their control card, seeing too few punches, and then saying, "sorry, I wasn't thirsty." Rick Hood
Course: 20 controls worth 1 point apiece, mass start, 45 minute time limit, 2 points per minute overtime penalty.
Meet Director: Mac MacDonald Courses: Ben Hall Registration: Kathy Trost Start: Ben Hall Finish: Ben Hall, Mac MacDonald

Sixteen lucky advanced-course orienteers were challenged to participate in a motala at Forest Park in Everett. A motala is an orienteering race in which all participants start at the same time, and each participant runs three to four short courses one after another -- but not necessarily in the same sequence as the other competitors. Each runner begins with one map, then trades it in for another when he/she completes the first course... and so on, until all the courses are completed.

At Forest Park there were four courses -- A, B, C and D -- and so while runner #1 might run the race in sequence ABCD, another might run DCBA, or BACD, etc. Ideally, the course sequences diverge as quickly as possible, so that runners who start the race on the same leg -- say, leg A -- will run a different 2nd leg. In practice, a four-course motala has only 24 discrete course combinations (4 factorial), and half of those have the same 1st/2nd leg combination as the other half... so if there are more than 12 participants, some people will be running the same 2 first legs as some others.

Rather than lose sleep over the combinatorics of motala sequences, I wrote a program to run on a laptop that would, among other things, automate the process of assigning course sequences to runners as they registered, insuring the maximum randomness for a given number of entrants. How well did it work? It doled out sequences CDAB and CDBA to John Rogers and Dave Enger, two people with exceptional and comparable orienteering skills; I couldn't have chosen a worse pair of sequences for these two. This explains why Microsoft returned my resume.

However, the program was useful in other areas -- most notably, in that I could easily take split times for the runners as they entered the map-exchange area in between legs. Motala splits give an interesting view into the mid-race dynamics by providing four mini-race results (see results). Here are some of my observations on that data:

All four legs were roughly 1.4k, for a total distance of just under 6k. John Rogers (a recent transplant from New England and 1990 U.S. Champion in M35), won the race in 50:48, by a scant 26 seconds over Dave Enger. Although John never posted the fastest time for any one leg, he was consistently 2nd or 3rd. Enger lost the race on leg B alone; his ACD time was better than John's. This is
